# UC-17.3.24 · Conditional Access Policy Enforcement (Entra ID)

## Description

Volume of grants vs blocks per named CA policy — complements generic UC-17.3.1 with Microsoft-specific policy dimension.

## Value

Volume of grants vs blocks per named CA policy — complements generic UC-17.3.1 with Microsoft-specific policy dimension.

## Implementation

Include `failureReason` for blocks. Alert when block rate for a policy jumps without change ticket.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Azure / Entra TA.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=azure:signin` with `conditional_access_status`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Include `failureReason` for blocks. Alert when block rate for a policy jumps without change ticket.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=identity sourcetype="azure:signin" earliest=-7d
| where isnotnull(conditional_access_policy_name)
| stats count by conditional_access_policy_name, conditional_access_status
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Conditional Access Policy Enforcement (Entra ID)** — Volume of grants vs blocks per named CA policy — complements generic UC-17.3.1 with Microsoft-specific policy dimension.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=azure:signin` with `conditional_access_status`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Azure / Entra TA. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: identity; **sourcetype**: azure:signin. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=identity, sourcetype="azure:signin", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(conditional_access_policy_name)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by conditional_access_policy_name, conditional_access_status** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the vendor admin console that produced the feed (see Data sources and App) for the same entities and time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize

Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Stacked bar (policy × status), Table (top blocks), Line chart (blocks / day per policy).

## SPL

```spl
index=identity sourcetype="azure:signin" earliest=-7d
| where isnotnull(conditional_access_policy_name)
| stats count by conditional_access_policy_name, conditional_access_status
| sort -count
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src span=1h
| sort - count
```

## Visualization

Stacked bar (policy × status), Table (top blocks), Line chart (blocks / day per policy).
